title: exec: retry rm -rf on ENOTEMPTY and EBUSY
body:
when running on NFS, a RemoveAll could cause EBUSY because of some unlinked files that are still kept open and "silly renamed" to .nfs$ID.
    
This is only half of the fix, as conmon needs to be fixed too.
    
Closes: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2040379
Related: https://github.com/containers/conmon/pull/319
    
[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED] as it requires NFS as the underlying storage.
    
Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Scrivano <gscrivan@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *giuseppe* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [giuseppe] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
LGTM and happy green test buttons.  I would like a @mheon head nod to make sure his concerns were addressed.

I'm still really iffy about this one. syncContainer() helps, but again, a lot of codepaths don't do any runtime-state checks after the initial sync-and-lock at the top of the function, and there's a real potential we break something if we unlock in a utility function like this. It's a little different for more top-level functions like stop(), which does unlock, but also has all the logic to handle the unlock in the same place.

without unlocking the container, I've seen two podman instances (the cleanup and the one doing `podman exec ..`) getting stuck on the same loop when the underlying storage is on NFS.  I don't think we will hit the fallback cleanup when using a local file system

Fortunately, there aren't that many uses of `cleanupExecBundle` so I've been able to look over all of them. container_exec.go line 941 looks potentially dangerous, because we're making changes to the DB that aren't being saved before the lock is yielded, and another Podman could get in and run a command in the meantime; I think we'll need a `c.save()` right before that line to prevent such a case.(iff `needSave` is true). 

The use on line 979, still container_exec.go, is somewhat more problematic, as the function it's in, `removeAllExecSession`, is called by the container removal function; I think we need to do a double-check of container state after calling it - runtime_ctr.go line 682 needs to verify that c.state.State is not `ContainerStateRunning` or `ContainerStatePaused` in order to continue, otherwise we'll set the Removing state on a running container, which could jam up container removal and prevent it from working unless the container is manually killed by the user.
Oh, and a comment on `cleanupExecBundle` that it unlocks/relocks the container, and should be used with care, would be much appreciated, we have to be careful how and where we use this one now.

thanks for the review and detailed description.

Is there any downside if we just call `c.save()` before `c.lock.Unlock()` in cleanupExecBundle()?  I'd not be too much concerned about performance, since this would affect cleanup on NFS only on rare race conditions with the podman cleanup process

> Calling `c.save()` there SGTM

thanks, fixed now
I think you still need to add the state checks to `removeContainer` - that needs actual verification

> I think you still need to add the state checks to `removeContainer` - that needs actual verification

changed the order so that removeAllExecSession is called when the container is in state `Removing`
